item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot here to like! There's a modesty of scale in this that I appreciate. If you're looking to improve it, I might suggest:

  • the Risk Class seems a bit low for something with this sort of life-destroying capabilities. On the other hand, if this is meant to be a subtle clue that something's not quite right about the way it has been described, then it works really well! So it might be better after all to keep it where it is.
  • I'd personally like just a bit more of a sense of what actually befell poor old Clementine. It's easy to end up on the side of showing-too-much, so I have some sympathy in erring on the side of not showing anything. But I still feel like I'd like just a glimpse of the real reasons why the researcher needed dismissal.
  • Some sort of redacted-placeholder-image, perhaps with 'THIS SPACE LEFT INTENTIONALLY BLANK', might be nice. Or it might be too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So Clementine wrote about breaking into 5183's containment unit and forging entries to fool his psychologist in the journal he was submitting to his psychologist? And the Foundation actually wrote that pretending to be Clementine? Even with the employee dismissal cover twist this doesn't seem to make a lot of sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is a minor critique on my part, but i think a simple fix would be…
replacing this phrase:
"who do not receive an amnestic within three hours of exposure begin suffering from high amounts of stress which result in hallucinations and intense paranoia within a few days."
with this phrase:
"where if not receiving an amnestic within three hours of exposure, begin suffering from high amounts of stress, which result in hallucinations and intense paranoia within a few days."
…since (imo) the original phrase had a bit of funny wording, and its lack of commas made it a bit "blocky" to read
if i form any more complex critiques over time, ill post them here, but that is all i have to suggest for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay let's see what you got,

crit
boop

…every physical manner1 when directly viewed by a living organism. SCP-5183 instances are also able to transform their appearance when not directly observed in order to better blend into their environment.

Contradictory statements. I'd remove, "when directly viewed by a living organism", and then reword the second sentence. These are just objects that can transform and observation doesn't really have anything to do with it.

C - The skull of a small animal, believed to be a squirrel.

Is the skull not complete? I feel like the foundation could figure out its species from a full skull. If it's not I'd specify that.

The affected individuals, hereafter referred to as SCP-5183-1 instances, who do not receive an amnestic within three hours of exposure begin suffering from high amounts of stress which result in hallucinations and intense paranoia within a few days.

This is a pretty good example of the type of clunky sentences that can be found throughout the piece.

No known treatment has been found to cure an instance of SCP-5183-1 after this point.

Choppy sentence/non-clinical

All known SCP-5183 instances were found in the former residence of PoI-5183, Ramona Jimenez, along with several texts on the subject of thaumaturgy. PoI-5813 is believed to be a member of the Daughters of Eden.

This is fairly choppy, but also kinda a weak discovery section, and it really doesn't add anything except the name drop of the Daughters of Eden. Not a deal-breaker, but more could've been done here

Researcher Clementine had already been keeping a journal of his experiences, at his psychologist's request.

Whenever possible you want to avoid using 's in the main document (for clinical reasons). So something like ", at the request of his psychologist." would work better here.

And really anything else to point out is just weird phrasing similar to what I've mentioned previously.

So, overall, I'm unfortunately not a fan, the anomaly itself is fairly boring (by design I'm sure) and that would be okay but the narrative is likewise boring/nonsensical. I can't think of a reason why the foundation needs to go through all this trouble for a cover story. They can just make one up, or not, but falsifying a document to make a cover story for someone being too stressed seems illogical. This is compounded by the notes which leave no clues that this could be false information, so the reveal just falls flat and we don't make a connection with the guy because we think its a compulsion. Which brings me to my final point: I have no clue what I'm supposed to feel. After reading this I didn't find it funny, sad, scary, heartwarming, or anything really– it just kinda "is". Unfortunately, it's going to be a -1 from me, I could see something being done with this concept, but it would need to be fleshed out a lot more in my opinion.



Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to DrAkimoto or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I reposted this article after a day because of some nice feedback I only got after posting. Thank you to everyone who helped critique this piece, including Cybersqyd, ThisMightBeAuto, Redesert, and OCuin.
